title: Fixing doctors’ charges is simple: restore the patients’ rebates
full_text:
Federal Health Minister Mark Butler has stated that medical specialist fees are out of control. He has called it “a barbecue stopper” and pledged to fix it.
Mr Butler is correct that out-of-pocket costs to see doctors represents another example of the increased cost of living, and there are cases of patients deferring essential procedures because of their inability to wear gap payments.
* Specialist fee hikes are easily outstripping inflation
Where we should all agree is that patients (and indeed consumers of any product) are entitled to transparency over fees. Providing informed financial consent is not only good manners, anything less is not ethical practice.
It is not fair when you have already established a relationship with a doctor, had a consultation, signed a consent form, and organised time off work or organised care for a dependent child or parent, to be told for the first time you will be thousands of dollars out of pocket.
Equally, it is simply not possible for doctors to provide an estimate for every single procedure prior to the consultation. There are numerous variables at play. You might not need surgery. Expectant or medical treatments might be more appropriate. Just because your mate from golf’s hip went well, does not mean that you have osteoarthritis and need an operation.
Some patients have “junk” insurance policies that, while sufficient to avoid the tax imposts levied on those above the relevant income thresholds, pay barely above the Medicare rebate.
The previous government set up a Medical Costs Finder website. I personally told former minister Greg Hunt the limitations of that system.
Firstly, it was meaningless for that large number of specialists who embrace the “no gap” and “known gap” schemes to publish a list of 20 fees for 20 different operations when the only relevant information is that the patient will pay a gap of $0 or somewhere up to $500, depending on their table of insurance and their provider.
Secondly, it should not be inferred that publishing fees will lead to reductions in cost. Let’s just say that on occasions I have become aware of colleagues less experienced or of lesser training than myself charging twice as much, and questioning where I set my fees.
Regrettably, many consumers (and this includes patients) equate a higher price with a better product.
The ability to compare and contrast fees seems desirable but you might just be comparing apples with dragonfruit. I might be able to do a hysterectomy just as well as a colleague. But if that hysterectomy is for a suspected cancer in a patient with multiple comorbidities it might fairly and reasonably cost more under the care of a different doctor.
Medical practice is not making widgets. It is complex and necessarily personal and individualised.
Mr Butler wants to publish all specialists’ fees. The ability to find out who is and who isn’t a “no gap” provider already exists. But predicting someone’s clinical course is not always feasible. Some patients will end up in a high-dependency unit. Others will see a physician as part of their care. The level and extent of anaesthetic involvement is variable.
Of course, improving fairness and transparency for common operations, e.g. knee replacements, cataracts and colonoscopies is highly desirable. No one would reasonably disagree with that.
The next issue is the fees themselves. Governments on both sides of politics have failed to index the Medicare Benefits Schedule anywhere near inflation for more than a generation.
Most galling was the Gillard government’s decision to freeze Medicare rebates in 2013.
The policy was continued by the Abbott government and only unwound by Mr Hunt and the Turnbull government in the year after they got the shock of their life, barely being returned to power in the “Mediscare” election of 2018.
Rebates for pathology and diagnostic imaging were frozen for even longer.
Government cannot blame doctors for setting fees above the woefully inadequate rebates paid for many procedures, and doing so fails to understand the cost pressures on our small businesses. We have staff to pay and other fixed costs including rent, utilities, insurance and consumables, all of which continue to rise in cost with the inflationary pressures this government has been failing to address long before trouble in the Strait of Hormuz.
When I was president of the Australian Medical Association, I never sought to defend egregious fee setting. I got into trouble with some colleagues when I said that I had never met a surgeon worth $20,000 for an afternoon’s work.
Some of the most gifted surgeons and procedural physicians in this country work in the public system for an hourly wage or in private practice for a fee at or below the AMA rate. But these are “false flag” arguments used by governments on both sides of politics who have failed to fund our health system.
We’ve had parliamentary inquiries in the past. Then-health minister Michael Wooldridge walked away from the Relative Value Study in 2001 when he realised what outstanding value for money patients and government were getting from Australia’s doctors, including the hours of unpaid work we all do each week.
It’s just much easier to bash doctors than to get the budget in shape and index MBS rebates at the rate of (health) inflation.
The lazy Grattan Institute suggestion to strip Medicare rebates from specialists who charge excessive fees failed to understand the most basic premise. It is the patient’s rebate, not the doctor’s.
Because the rebate long ago lost anything like parity with the cost of delivering care for many procedures, there is a “gap” between what Medicare (and the private health insurers) pay and what the true value is.
The government’s attempt to streamline the ability of international medical graduates to work in Australia will not address out-of-pocket costs. Private medical care is an inelastic market and there are numerous examples where a modest gap results in more effective use of health services.
It is essential that Australians retain choice over where they receive their healthcare. There will always be a proportion of the population who do not have the means to exercise a choice. Waiting times for public hospital outpatient appointments and for elective surgery should be addressed at the same time.
I dream of the day that state and territory and federal health ministers stop blaming each other for areas where public hospitals fail Australians. Blaming doctors for the inability to fund the MBS is every bit as insincere and false.
The decision to cruel higher private health insurance rebates for over-65s will only add to the cost of private care and the waiting times in our public system. Seventy per cent of elective surgery occurs in private hospitals. Public waiting lists are already growing year on year before that attack on the private hospital system.
Instead of looking at ways to control doctors’ fees, the overwhelming majority of which are fair and reasonable, Mr Butler could put back all of the $5bn-$6bn the AMA estimates have been taken out of the pockets of ordinary Australians with the freeze on Medicare rebates.
Stop blaming doctors. Deliver the necessary “catch up” and then index in line with inflation. It’s so simple. Surely it’s within their reach.
Dr Michael Gannon is a consultant obstetrician and gynaecologist with 20 years’ experience as a specialist. He has delivered more than 6000 babies. He served as president of the AMA from 2016 to 2018. He is president of leading professional indemnity provider MDA National.
